# Enhancing Fraud Detection Using Synthetic Transactions Generated by Gen AI

**Domain:** Finance | **Project type:** Data Generation & Augmentation using Generative AI
**Final Project — Six-Week AI-ML Internship (GNCIPL)**

## What this is

A rigorous, honest test of whether generative-AI-based synthesis of the minority (fraud)
class improves a fraud classifier — run on the **genuine Kaggle "Credit Card Fraud
Detection" dataset** (284,807 transactions, 492 real fraud cases), with leak-free
methodology at every stage. See `Final_Report.docx` / `.pdf` for the full write-up and
`Fraud_Detection_Slides.pptx` for the stakeholder summary.

**Headline result — read this before quoting a single number:** at the classifier's
default threshold, augmenting training data with 5,000 synthetic fraud transactions
raised recall by +6.8 points (75.7% → 82.4%) but slightly *reduced* F1 (-1.8 points) as
precision fell. At matched precision/recall operating points, the two models are close to
a wash (Average Precision 0.822 vs. 0.819). **The more useful finding:** an earlier run of
this identical pipeline under severe data scarcity (80 real fraud rows instead of 344)
produced a dramatic, unambiguous improvement (+44.1 points of recall). Put together: this
technique's value depends heavily on how much real minority-class data you already have.
Full detail in Section 6.3 of the report.

## One substitution made in this build, and how to undo it

This pipeline runs in a sandboxed environment with **no disk space for a PyTorch/CTGAN
install**. One swap was made:

| Original plan | What this build uses instead | How to switch back |
|---|---|---|
| CTGAN (`sdv`/`ctgan`, requires PyTorch) | Gaussian Copula (`copulas.multivariate.GaussianMultivariate`) — explicitly listed as a valid technique in the project brief's own "Choose a Gen AI Method" table | `pip install sdv`, then in `Notebooks/2_CTGAN_Training.ipynb` replace `GaussianMultivariate` with `sdv.single_table.CTGANSynthesizer` (exact code in Appendix B of the report). Worth trying — the moderate-data result was close enough that a higher-fidelity generator could plausibly tip it. |

The dataset itself **is** the genuine Kaggle dataset (obtained via a GitHub-hosted mirror,
since kaggle.com is not reachable from this sandbox — see Appendix C of the report for
provenance).

## Folder structure

```
Synthetic-Fraud-AI-Project/
├── Final_Report.docx / .pdf      Full project report (cover page -> appendix)
├── Fraud_Detection_Slides.pptx   10-slide stakeholder presentation
├── Notebooks/
│   ├── 1_EDA.ipynb                Exploratory data analysis on the real dataset (pre-run)
│   ├── 2_CTGAN_Training.ipynb     Leak-free generative synthesis (pre-run)
│   ├── 3_Model_Training.ipynb     Baseline vs. augmented model training (pre-run)
│   └── 4_Visualization.ipynb      Full evaluation incl. matched-threshold + scarcity finding
├── Data/
│   ├── creditcard.csv              The genuine Kaggle dataset — 284,807 rows
│   ├── synthetic_fraud.csv         5,000 Gen-AI-generated synthetic fraud rows
│   ├── augmented_dataset.csv       Training-only real+synthetic combination
│   ├── model_comparison_metrics.csv
│   ├── model_comparison_summary.json   (includes matched-threshold + scarcity-comparison numbers)
│   ├── synthesis_quality_report.csv
│   └── plots/                      All chart PNGs used in the report/slides/notebooks
├── Model/
│   └── fraud_model_rf.pkl          Trained Random Forest (augmented model)
└── README.md                       This file
```

## Re-running the pipeline

```bash
pip install pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn copulas joblib
jupyter notebook Notebooks/1_EDA.ipynb   # then 2, 3, 4 in order
```

Note: `RandomForestClassifier` is set to `n_estimators=100, max_depth=16` — capped for
training speed on this environment's single CPU core (~150s per model). On a normal
multi-core machine you can safely raise both for a stronger model.

To load the trained model elsewhere:
```python
import joblib
model = joblib.load("Model/fraud_model_rf.pkl")
model.predict(new_transactions_df[FEATURE_COLUMNS])  # Time, V1..V28, Amount
```

## Two methodology points worth knowing

1. **Classifier leak-free split:** the real data is split into train/test **before** any
   synthetic rows are created, and the test set is never touched by synthetic data.
2. **Generator leak-free fitting:** the Gaussian Copula generative model is fit **only** on
   the 344 fraud rows in the *training* split — never on all 492 real fraud rows. Fitting
   it on the full set (including the 148 test-set fraud rows) would let the generator
   indirectly "see" the test data's statistical shape before evaluation — an easy-to-miss
   leakage risk that this project deliberately avoids.

Both matter: skipping either would have made the results look better than they honestly are.

## Key limitations to disclose alongside any of these numbers

- Gaussian Copula, not full CTGAN, generated the synthetic rows (see substitution table above).
- Random Forest depth was capped for single-core training speed, not tuned for best performance.
- The "scarcity matters" finding compares two runs of one pipeline on related data — a real,
  informative signal, but not yet confirmed against a second independent dataset.
- Precision drops as recall rises at the default threshold — normal for this kind of
  rebalancing; tune the classification threshold to your actual cost of a false positive
  vs. a missed fraud rather than assuming augmentation is a free win.

Full detail on all of the above is in `Final_Report.docx`, Sections 1, 6, and 8.
